body: Integration by parts is the reverse of the product rule, allowing us to transform an integral of a product into a simpler one. Mastery hinges on a systematic choice of *u* and *dv*, followed by disciplined algebraic manipulation. This lesson unpacks the selection strategies, step‑by‑step execution, and the power of repeated application.

text: Key Takeaway: Use the LIATE (Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential) hierarchy to pick *u* first; *dv* is whatever remains.

title: Paul's Online Math Notes – Integration by Parts
relevance: Provides the formal statement of the IBP formula, detailed examples, and guidance on choosing *u* and *dv*.

title: Krista King Math – How to Pick *u* and *dv* (LIATE/LIPET)
relevance: Explains the LIATE/LIPET rule with practical tips and common pitfalls when selecting *u*.

items: Identify the integrand as a product *f(x)·g(x)* that resists direct integration.
Apply the LIATE (or LIPET) rule to choose *u* – the factor that simplifies when differentiated.
Set *dv* to the remaining factor; integrate it to obtain *v*.
Differentiate *u* to get *du* and substitute into the IBP formula ∫u dv = uv – ∫v du.
Simplify the resulting integral; if it still contains a product, repeat the process.
Combine like terms and add the constant of integration.

body: Solve the integral ∫x e^x dx in two ways: (a) using integration by parts as outlined above, and (b) by recognizing it as the derivative of x e^x – e^x and integrating directly. Compare the algebraic effort and insight each method provides.

questions.q: For the integral ∫ln(x) dx, which choice follows the LIATE rule?
questions.explanation: Logarithmic functions rank highest in LIATE, so u = ln(x) and dv = dx.

questions.q: After choosing u = x and dv = e^x dx, what is v?
questions.explanation: Integrating dv = e^x dx yields v = e^x.

questions.q: When the resulting integral after one IBP still contains a product, what should you do?
questions.explanation: Repeated application of IBP is the standard strategy for nested products.

title: Area Swapping Visualization
caption: Interactive demo showing how the area under u·dv is transformed into uv minus the area under v·du, illustrating the geometric intuition behind integration by parts.

title: Ask the Tutor
greeting: Hello! I'm Professor Alvarez. Need a hint on picking *u* or simplifying the resulting integral? Ask me anything about integration by parts.
